questions.question: What is the difference between intermediate and terminal operations in Streams? What is lazy evaluation?
questions.quickAnswer: Intermediate operations (filter, map, sorted) return a Stream and are lazy — they don't execute until a terminal operation is called. Terminal operations (collect, forEach, count, findFirst) trigger the pipeline. Lazy evaluation means operations on elements that aren't needed (e.g., findFirst found after 3 elements) are never executed for the remaining elements.
keyPoints: Intermediate: filter, map, flatMap, sorted, distinct, peek, limit, skip — return Stream, lazy
Terminal: collect, forEach, count, findFirst, findAny, anyMatch, reduce, toList() — trigger execution
Lazy: filter().map() chains don't loop until terminal is hit — single pass through elements
Short-circuit: findFirst/anyMatch stop as soon as result is found
Stateless: filter, map — don't need to see all elements; Stateful: sorted, distinct — buffer all elements
Parallel: stream().parallel() — splits into sub-tasks via ForkJoinPool; order not guaranteed without forEachOrdered()
Cannot reuse a stream — once terminal is called, stream is closed

questions.question: What is the difference between map() and flatMap() in streams?
questions.quickAnswer: map() transforms each element 1-to-1 and wraps the result — if the mapper returns a List, you get a Stream<List>. flatMap() transforms each element to a Stream and flattens them into one stream — if mapper returns a List, you get the elements directly. Use flatMap() when your mapping function returns a collection.
keyPoints: map(f): Stream<T> → Stream<R> — one output per input
flatMap(f): Stream<T> → Stream<R> — zero or more outputs per input, flattened
Example: words.map(w -> w.split('')) → Stream<String[]>; flatMap → Stream<String>
Optional.flatMap: avoids nested Optional<Optional<T>> — if mapper returns Optional<R>
CompletableFuture.thenCompose vs thenApply: same pattern (flatMap vs map for async)
Use case: orders → each order has items → flatMap to get all items across all orders
Performance: flatMap with large sub-streams is fine; avoid when output is always single element (use map)

questions.question: What is the difference between synchronized, ReentrantLock, and ReadWriteLock?
questions.quickAnswer: synchronized is implicit (using object monitor), non-interruptible, and has no timeout. ReentrantLock is explicit (lock()/unlock() in try-finally), supports tryLock(timeout), lockInterruptibly(), and fairness. ReadWriteLock allows multiple concurrent readers or one exclusive writer — dramatically improves read-heavy workloads.
keyPoints: synchronized: simpler, auto-releases, cannot try without blocking, no condition variables (only wait/notify)
ReentrantLock: tryLock(1, SECONDS), lockInterruptibly(), fair=true (FIFO queue)
Always: lock.lock(); try { ... } finally { lock.unlock(); } — never skip unlock()
ReentrantReadWriteLock: readLock() for reads (shared), writeLock() for writes (exclusive)
ReadWriteLock benefit: 10 threads can read simultaneously; one write blocks all readers
ReentrantLock condition: lock.newCondition(); cond.await(); cond.signal() — like wait/notify but per-condition
StampedLock (Java 8): optimistic reads — try read without lock, validate stamp, re-read if invalidated

questions.question: What is the Executor framework? What are the differences between thread pool types?
questions.quickAnswer: The Executor framework decouples task submission from execution. Key factory methods: newFixedThreadPool(n) — bounded threads, queued tasks; newCachedThreadPool() — unbounded threads, reused; newSingleThreadExecutor() — sequential; newScheduledThreadPool() — delayed/periodic. Always shutdown() the pool to release threads.
keyPoints: Executors.newFixedThreadPool(n): n threads, LinkedBlockingQueue (unbounded!) — beware OOM from queue growth
Executors.newCachedThreadPool(): unlimited threads (risk of OOM/thread explosion under burst load)
Executors.newSingleThreadExecutor(): single thread, tasks execute in submission order
Executors.newScheduledThreadPool(n): scheduleAtFixedRate() or scheduleWithFixedDelay()
Virtual threads (Java 21): Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor() — preferred for I/O-heavy
ThreadPoolExecutor: full control — core/max pool size, keep-alive, queue type, rejection policy
RejectedExecutionPolicy: AbortPolicy (throw), CallerRunsPolicy (submit thread runs it), DiscardPolicy

questions.question: What is CompletableFuture? How is it different from Future?
questions.quickAnswer: Future is a placeholder for an async result — you can only get() (blocking) or cancel(). CompletableFuture adds non-blocking composition: thenApply (map), thenCompose (flatMap), thenCombine (zip), allOf (wait for all), anyOf (first done). Callbacks are called when the future completes without blocking.
keyPoints: Future.get() blocks the calling thread — useless for async pipelines
CompletableFuture.thenApply(fn): apply fn when result is ready (like Stream.map)
thenCompose(fn): fn returns CompletableFuture — flattens (like Stream.flatMap)
thenCombine(cf2, fn): combine two independent futures when both complete
CompletableFuture.allOf(cf1, cf2, cf3): returns CF<Void> that completes when all do
supplyAsync(supplier): run on ForkJoinPool.commonPool(); supplyAsync(s, executor): custom pool
exceptionally(ex -> fallback): handle exception without interrupting the chain
whenComplete(fn): always runs (success and failure) — like finally

questions.question: What is a deadlock? How do you prevent it in Java?
questions.quickAnswer: Deadlock occurs when two or more threads each hold a lock the other needs, causing permanent blocking. Prevention strategies: consistent lock ordering (always acquire locks in the same order), use tryLock with timeout, avoid nested synchronized blocks, use higher-level abstractions (java.util.concurrent).
keyPoints: Four conditions for deadlock: mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait
Break circular wait: always acquire lock A before lock B — all threads follow same order
ReentrantLock.tryLock(timeout): gives up if can't acquire — no circular wait possible
Detect: thread dump (jstack/jcmd) shows 'Found deadlock' with involved threads
Avoid: minimize lock scope, avoid calling external methods while holding lock (alien method)
Livelock: threads keep yielding/retrying to each other — look busy but make no progress
Java thread dump: kill -3 on Linux or jstack PID — includes 'Deadlock found' section

questions.question: What is the difference between Runnable, Callable, and Future in Java concurrency?
questions.quickAnswer: Runnable has run() — no return value, can't throw checked exceptions. Callable<V> has call() — returns a value and can throw checked exceptions. Future<V> is the result holder for a Callable submitted to ExecutorService — get() blocks until done, get(timeout) is bounded.
keyPoints: Runnable: void run() — fire-and-forget tasks, submit to executor or Thread.start()
Callable<V>: V call() throws Exception — result-returning tasks
Future<V>: executor.submit(callable) returns Future; future.get() blocks for result
Future.isDone(): non-blocking check; future.cancel(mayInterrupt): attempt cancellation
ExecutorService.invokeAll(callables): submit all, wait for all, returns List<Future<V>>
ExecutorService.invokeAny(callables): returns first successful result, cancels others
CompletableFuture replaces Future for non-blocking composition (thenApply, thenCompose)

questions.question: What is ThreadLocal? What are the risks of using it in thread pools?
questions.quickAnswer: ThreadLocal provides a per-thread variable — each thread gets its own copy, invisible to other threads. Risk in thread pools: pool threads are reused, so ThreadLocal values set in one request persist for the next request served by the same thread. Always call remove() in a finally block after each task completes.
keyPoints: ThreadLocal<T>: set(value), get(), remove() — separate instance per thread
Use case: security context (Spring SecurityContextHolder), request correlation (MDC), DB connection per thread
Thread pool reuse problem: TaskA sets threadLocal=userA, completes, TaskB runs on same thread, reads userA!
Fix: always threadLocal.remove() in finally after task completion
Spring RequestContextHolder, MDC, SecurityContextHolder all use ThreadLocal internally
InheritableThreadLocal: child threads inherit parent's value (use case: trace ID propagation)
Memory leak: ThreadLocal key is weakly referenced; value is strongly referenced — GC can't collect value

questions.question: What are the Java 8 Stream Collectors? Name five commonly used ones.
questions.quickAnswer: Collectors are terminal operations for collect(). Key ones: toList(), toSet(), toMap(keyFn, valueFn), groupingBy(classifier), joining(delimiter), counting(), partitioningBy(predicate), toUnmodifiableList(). groupingBy is the most powerful — groups elements into a Map<K, List<V>>.
keyPoints: Collectors.toList(): collect to ArrayList; Collectors.toUnmodifiableList() (Java 10+) for immutable
Collectors.toMap(k, v): careful with duplicate keys — third merge function required
Collectors.groupingBy(Person::getDept): Map<String, List<Person>> — department groups
Collectors.groupingBy(dept, Collectors.counting()): Map<String, Long> — count per group
Collectors.joining(', ', '[', ']'): 'hello, world' with delimiter, prefix, suffix
Collectors.partitioningBy(pred): Map<Boolean, List<T>> — true/false split
Downstream collectors: groupingBy(dept, averagingInt(Person::getSalary))

questions.question: What is Optional<T>? When should you use it and when should you avoid it?
questions.quickAnswer: Optional<T> is a container that may or may not contain a value, replacing null checks with a fluent API. Use it as return type for methods that may produce no result. Avoid it as: method parameter (use overloading), field type (serialization issues), or inside Collections. Never call get() without isPresent() — use orElse/orElseGet/orElseThrow instead.
keyPoints: Optional.of(value): throws NPE if null; Optional.ofNullable(value): wraps null as empty
orElse(default): returns default if empty (always evaluates default expression)
orElseGet(supplier): lazy default — only called if empty (prefer for expensive defaults)
orElseThrow(Exception::new): throw if empty — replaces manual null check + throw
map(fn): transform if present, stays Optional; flatMap(fn): when fn returns Optional
ifPresent(consumer): run side-effect if present; ifPresentOrElse(fn, emptyFn) Java 9+
Anti-pattern: opt.isPresent() then opt.get() — use orElse/map instead

questions.question: What is the difference between String, StringBuilder, and StringBuffer?
questions.quickAnswer: String is immutable — every operation creates a new object. StringBuilder is mutable, not thread-safe, O(1) amortized append. StringBuffer is mutable, thread-safe (synchronized methods), but slower. Use StringBuilder in single-threaded code; use String.join/Collectors.joining for stream assembly; avoid String + in loops.
keyPoints: String immutable: 'hello' + 'world' creates a new String; compiler optimizes string literals
String + in loop: O(n²) total — each += creates new String copying all previous content
StringBuilder: append() is amortized O(1), toString() at end — use in loops
StringBuffer: same API as StringBuilder, every method synchronized — use only if shared across threads
String.format() / formatted(): readable but creates intermediate strings — use for readability, not performance
String pool: literals are interned; new String('x') is not — always use .equals() not ==
Java 11 String methods: isBlank(), strip(), repeat(n), lines() returning Stream<String>

questions.question: What is try-with-resources and how does it work with AutoCloseable?
questions.quickAnswer: try-with-resources automatically calls close() on resources declared in the try header, in reverse order, even if an exception is thrown. The resource class must implement AutoCloseable. Multiple resources are closed in reverse declaration order. Suppressed exceptions (from close()) are attached to the primary exception.
keyPoints: AutoCloseable: one method — void close() throws Exception
Closeable extends AutoCloseable: close() throws IOException — for I/O resources
try (Resource r = new Resource()) { ... } — r.close() called automatically after try block
Multiple resources: try (A a = ...; B b = ...) — B closed first, then A (reverse order)
Suppressed exceptions: if both try block and close() throw, close() exception is suppressed
Access suppressed: e.getSuppressed() on the primary exception
Java 9: effectively final variables as resources — try (existing) { } without re-declaration
